text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: History of seizures with predominantly nocturnal events per partner reports; mood stable; improving sleep hygiene; no recent intercurrent illness; smoker attempting reduction. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Vitamin D 800 IU daily.  

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today. He attended alone; his partner, who usually observes night-time episodes, was not present at today’s review. He reports feeling well, sleeping more regularly, and using a phone alarm to prompt evening medication. He denies daytime spells, blackouts, or lapses in awareness, and reports no injuries, tongue biting, or nocturnal confusion on waking over the past year. He has returned to part‑time work and is cycling short distances. Mood is stable; no recent alcohol excess. 

Seizures: Historically, episodes were predominantly nocturnal and partner‑witnessed, with brief post‑event fatigue on waking. In the absence of the partner today we relied on the patient’s diary, pharmacy collection records, and the last community epilepsy nurse summary. These all concord with his report of sustained stability. No events for twelve months. He has not required rescue medication, and there have been no emergency attendances. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves normal. Power full and symmetrical in all limbs. Coordination and gait normal. No focal deficits. 

Investigations: Last routine bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) six months ago were within reference ranges. Lamotrigine trough level not routinely monitored; no clinical indication to check today. Prior MRI (2022) unchanged; prior EEG (2023) non‑localising. 

Plan:  
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily with ongoing adherence prompts.  
- Reinforce sleep hygiene and avoidance of sleep deprivation, given prior nocturnal pattern.  
- Provide letter for employer confirming stable seizure control and no restrictions beyond standard safety precautions.  
- Safety: continue partner‑kept overnight diary; advise to video any future events if safe.  
- Routine bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D) via GP in the next 3–4 months or sooner if clinical change.  
- Review in clinic in 9–12 months, earlier if any recurrence or concern.  

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "No events for twelve months." This indicates the patient has had no seizures for at least 12 months. The criteria for seizure free status (no seizures for at least 6 months) are met. Therefore, the normalized label is 'seizure free for 12 month'. No other conflicting frequencies are provided.
